comment: Both are direct Abstract subtypes denoting representation-independent content, with near-identical framing (DOLCE: "purely combinatorial"; SUMO: "an abstraction that may have multiple representations"). The closest structural pairing in this crosswalk.

Both explicitly name and contrast the bearer/content distinction with near-identical worked examples (SUMO: "strings, sounds, icons"; YAMATO: "many ways to code the same content into different forms") — the strongest cross-source lexical and conceptual match of the three.

Same core distinction between DOLCE's and YAMATO's Proposition, directly and confidently compared. YAMATO's own further subdivision (representation-primary/-secondary, designed/product proposition) has no counterpart in DOLCE's single undifferentiated `proposition` class — a confirmed structural fact about YAMATO's additional tier, not an open sourcing question about the core Proposition-to-Proposition match itself, which is directly and clearly evidenced.
